items.title: Causative agent
items.contentHtml:
Fasciola hepatica

  • Fasciola hepatica is a digenean, hermaphroditic trematode, with a heteroxenous cycle that requires an intermediate host (snail of the genus Lymnaea).
  • Its adult form measures 2-3 cm, with a leaf shape and a ventral suction cup; It lives in the bile ducts of mammals.
  • The eggs are operculated, large (130-150 µm) and are eliminated with the feces of the definitive host.
  • Do not confuse with Fasciolopsis buski: this is intestinal, not biliary, and its eggs are morphologically indistinguishable, but the clinical and epidemiological context guides.

items.title: Biliary habitat
items.contentHtml:
Habitat in bile ducts

  • The adult of Fasciola hepatica resides in the major bile ducts and gallbladder, where it feeds on blood and epithelium.
  • Larval migration through the liver parenchyma (acute phase) causes necrosis, hemorrhage and tissue eosinophilia.
  • In the chronic phase, the presence of the parasite induces hyperplasia of the epithelium. biliary tract, wall thickening and periductal fibrosis (secondary sclerosing cholangitis).
  • Key fact: biliary obstruction can cause bacterial cholangitis and pigmentary lithiasis due to egg deposition.

items.title: animal reservoir
items.contentHtml:
Main reservoir

  • The main reservoir is sheep and cattle; also goats, buffaloes and other herbivores.
  • Humans are accidental hosts (zoonoses). Transmission occurs in areas where livestock graze in wetlands with infected snails.
  • Prevalence in animals can exceed 50% in endemic regions (Andean highlands, North Africa, Middle East).
  • In exams: they ask whether control of the reservoir (deworming of livestock) is a public health measure to reduce human incidence.

items.title: infective form
items.contentHtml:
Metacercaria

  • Metacercaria is the infective form for humans and livestock; It is ingested when consuming raw aquatic plants (cress, alfalfa, mint).
  • It encysts in leaves and stems; It is resistant to gastric acidity and excysts in the duodenum.
  • Metacercaria releases a larva that crosses the intestinal wall, migrates to the peritoneum, penetrates the liver capsule and reaches the parenchyma.
  • Trap: not to be confused with an embryonated egg (it is not infectious for humans); The egg in water gives rise to miracidium that infects the snail.

items.foco: Route of infection: ingestion of contaminated watercress
items.title: Route of infection
items.contentHtml:
Ingestion of contaminated watercress

  • The route of infection is the ingestion of contaminated watercress or other raw aquatic plants (for example alfalfa, water lettuce) that carry attached metacercariae.
  • Watercress (Nasturtium officinale) is the classic vehicle in endemic areas; Contamination occurs through livestock feces or infected snails in the culture water.
  • There is no person-to-person transmission, nor through filtered or boiled water (metacercariae are inactivated with heat >60°C).
  • In prep: if they ask for source, the answer is raw watercress; no meat or fish.

items.foco: Acute phase: invasive hepatitis or acute fascioliasis
items.title: Acute phase
items.contentHtml:
Acute fascioliasis

  • The acute phase corresponds to the migration of larvae through the liver parenchyma, producing invasive hepatitis with necrosis, marked eosinophilia (>50%) and pain in the right hypochondrium.
  • Clinic: fever, painful hepatomegaly, urticaria, severe eosinophilia (eosinophilia >5,000 cells/μL).
  • It can simulate amoebic or pyogenic liver abscess; but extreme eosinophilia is a key differential.
  • Serological diagnosis (ELISA) is positive in the acute phase before eggs appear in feces (prepatent period of 8-12 weeks).

items.foco: Chronic phase: cholangitis and biliary fibrosis
items.title: chronic phase
items.contentHtml:
Chronic biliary phase

  • The chronic phase is established when adult parasites reach the bile ducts, causing cholangitis and biliary fibrosis.
  • Clinic: recurrent biliary pain, cholestasis, intermittent jaundice, added bacterial cholangitis.
  • Imaging finding: thickening of the wall of the bile ducts, dilation of the duct intrahepatic biliary, and sometimes a “leaf” image on cholangiography.
  • Periductal fibrosis can progress to secondary biliary cirrhosis (rare). Eggs in feces appear in this phase (from 3-4 months post-infection).

items.foco: Diagnosis: eggs in feces or bile
items.title: Parasitological diagnosis
items.contentHtml:
Eggs in feces or bile

  • The definitive diagnosis is the identification of eggs in feces (sedimentation technique or Kato-Katz) or in bile obtained by duodenal drainage or cholangiography.
  • The eggs are large (130-150 μm), operculated, oval in shape and yellowish in content; They are differentiated from Fasciolopsis buski only by clinical context.
  • Multiple serial samples are required by intermittent elimination. Sensitivity increases with concentration techniques (Ritchie sedimentation).
  • In the acute phase (without eggs), the diagnosis is serological: ELISA for excretory/secretory antigen (sensitivity >90%).

items.title: Image test
items.contentHtml:
Cholangiogram or ultrasound

  • Abdominal ultrasound: can show dilation of the intrahepatic bile duct, thickening of the wall of the ducts and, occasionally, mobile echogenic images compatible with adult parasites.
  • Cholangiogram (endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography or cholangio-magnetic resonance imaging): shows elongated filling defects in bile ducts, sometimes with a “leaf” appearance or “fishbone”.
  • Cholangiography also allows extraction of the parasite for diagnostic and therapeutic purposes.
  • computed tomography shows hypodense areas in the liver parenchyma in the acute phase (migration paths) and biliary dilation in the chronic phase.

items.title: Treatment of choice
items.contentHtml:
Triclabendazole

  • Triclabendazole is the drug of choice for fascioliasis, active against adult and juvenile forms (unlike praziquantel, which is ineffective).
  • Dose: 10 mg/kg orally, in a single dose or two doses with an interval of 12-24 hours according to guidelines (WHO recommends repeating it after 12 hours in cases serious).
  • Efficacy >90% in chronic phase; In the acute phase, a second dose may be required after 7 days.
  • Do not use bithionol (withdrawn) or dehydroemetine (toxicity). Praziquantel is not effective for Fasciola hepatica.

items.title: Triclabendazole Dosage
items.contentHtml:
Dose of triclabendazole

  • The standard dose is 10 mg/kg orally, administered with food to improve absorption.
  • In severe acute infection or in areas of resistance, the WHO recommends two doses of 10 mg/kg 12 hours apart (total 20 mg/kg).
  • In children, adjust by weight; no adjustment required in hepatic or renal failure.
  • Adverse effects: abdominal pain, nausea, dizziness; rarely transient cholestasis due to massive death of parasites.

items.foco: Prevention: avoid raw watercress in endemic areas
items.title: Prevention
items.contentHtml:
Prevention

  • The main measure is avoid raw watercress and other raw aquatic plants in endemic areas (Andean highlands, Mediterranean basin, Asia).
  • Cooking of plants (>60°C) or disinfection with iodine (not chlorine) inactivates metacercariae.
  • Control of snails (molluscicides) and periodic deworming of livestock reduces environmental pollution.
  • Do not drink untreated water from streams or canals where livestock graze. Health education is key in rural communities.
